1: A shadow: YHWH spoke to Moses. The divine speech that opens the festival calendar introduces what Paul calls 'a shadow of things to come' (Colossians 2:16-17). YHWH himself schedules his appointments with humanity — the feasts are not human inventions but divine mo'adim (appointed meeting times).
2: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel and say to them: These are the appointed feasts [mo'adei] of YHWH that you shall proclaim as holy convocations; they are my appointed feasts.' Mo'ed means an appointed time and place of meeting — YHWH's standing appointments with his people. Paul: 'These are a shadow of the things to come, but the substance [sōma] belongs to Christ' (Colossians 2:17). The substance of every divine appointment in the feast calendar is Christ himself: at Passover, his death; at Firstfruits, his resurrection; at Pentecost, his Spirit; at the fall feasts, his return.
3: A direct type: 'Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day is a Sabbath of solemn rest, a holy convocation. You shall do no work. It is a Sabbath to YHWH in all your dwelling places.' The Sabbath opens the feast calendar. Christ declared himself 'Lord of the Sabbath' (Mark 2:28). His death-burial-resurrection spans the three-day creation-week pattern: crucified on the sixth day (Friday), rested in the tomb on the seventh day (Sabbath), rose on the first/eighth day. Hebrews applies the Sabbath typology to eschatological rest: 'there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God... whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his' (Hebrews 4:9-10).
4: A shadow: 'These are the appointed feasts of YHWH, the holy convocations, which you shall proclaim at the time appointed for them.' Introduction to the seven feasts proper. The structure — seven feasts in two groups (spring and fall, separated by a long summer) — anticipates the gap between Christ's first and second coming: the spring feasts (Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Pentecost) are fulfilled at the first coming; the fall feasts (Trumpets, Yom Kippur, Tabernacles) are eschatological.
5: A direct type: 'In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at twilight, is YHWH's Passover.' The Passover (Pesach) is the first and foundational feast, historically rooted in the Exodus but fulfilled at the cross. Paul: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7). John's gospel frames the crucifixion narrative around the Passover: 'it was the day of Preparation of the Passover' (John 19:14). Jesus died as the lambs were being slaughtered for Passover — the precision of the fulfillment is exact.
6: A direct type: 'And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the Feast of Unleavened Bread to YHWH; for seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.' The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins the day after Passover — the burial follows the death. Unleavened bread = without leaven = without corruption. Peter applies Psalms 16:10 to Christ's burial: 'you will not let your Holy One see corruption' (Acts 2:27). Christ's body was in the tomb during the feast of Unleavened Bread and did not decay — the feast of incorruption is the feast of his burial.
7: A shadow: 'On the first day you shall have a holy convocation; you shall not do any ordinary work.' The opening convocation of Unleavened Bread marks the beginning of the burial period. The disciples on Holy Saturday were in grief, keeping a kind of Sabbath of mourning — not understanding that this was not the end.
8: A shadow: 'But you shall present a food offering to YHWH for seven days. On the seventh day is a holy convocation; you shall not do any ordinary work.' The entire seven-day feast frame marks the burial period as consecrated — set apart for YHWH. Even in death, Christ's tomb is consecrated: 'he was in the tomb' (John 20:1-2), guarded, sealed — a holy place.
9: A shadow: YHWH spoke to Moses regarding the firstfruits. The divine introduction to the Firstfruits feast signals that what follows is not merely agricultural but revelatory — a prophetic marker of what God will do in history.
10: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, When you come into the land that I give you and reap its harvest, you shall bring the sheaf [omer] of the firstfruits of your harvest to the priest.' The firstfruits wave-sheaf (omer reshit) is brought from the new harvest — the first portion, before anyone eats the new crop. Paul: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep... each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ' (1 Corinthians 15:20, 23). Christ's resurrection is the wave-sheaf: the first portion of the resurrection harvest, offered to God, guaranteeing the full harvest to follow.
11: A direct type: 'And he shall wave the sheaf before YHWH, so that you may be accepted. On the day after the Sabbath the priest shall wave it.' 'The day after the Sabbath' — the first day of the new week — is Sunday. The Firstfruits offering is to be waved on Sunday. Christ rose 'on the first day of the week' (Matthew 28:1; Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1; John 20:1) — on the exact day specified for the wave-offering of firstfruits. The timing of the resurrection fulfills the Firstfruits feast to the day: Sunday, the day after the Sabbath, is the day the first wave-sheaf is lifted before God.
12: A direct type: 'And on the day when you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a male lamb a year old without blemish [tamim] as a burnt offering to YHWH.' The lamb without blemish accompanies the firstfruits wave-sheaf. The Lamb of God (John 1:29) accompanies his own resurrection: Christ's perfect sacrifice and his resurrection are inseparable — the resurrection is the Father's acceptance of the perfect sacrifice (Romans 4:25: 'raised for our justification').
13: A shadow: The grain offering and drink offering accompanying the Firstfruits. The full suite of offerings at the first-fruits presentation — lamb, grain, oil, wine — reflects the comprehensive nature of the new creation inaugurated by resurrection: all of life (grain, wine, oil) renewed in Christ's rising.
14: A direct type: 'And you shall eat neither bread nor grain parched or fresh until this same day, until you have brought the offering of your God: it is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwellings.' The new grain cannot be eaten until the firstfruits are presented. The new covenant harvest — Pentecost and all that follows — could not begin until the resurrection of Christ (the firstfruits) was accomplished. Acts 1:4: 'he ordered them not to depart from Jerusalem, but to wait for the promise of the Father.' The disciples waited for the firstfruits to be accepted before the harvest could proceed.
15: A direct type: 'You shall count seven full weeks from the day after the Sabbath, from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering.' Pentecost (Shavuot) is counted from the resurrection Sunday. The count begins at Christ's resurrection. Acts 1-2: from the resurrection to Pentecost is exactly the 49-day count of Shavuot. The disciples waited, counted, and on the 50th day the Spirit came — the fulfillment arrived precisely on schedule.
16: A direct type: 'You shall count fifty days to the day after the seventh Sabbath. Then you shall present a grain offering of new grain to YHWH.' The 50-day count lands on Pentecost — pentēkostē (Greek for 50th). Acts 2:1: 'When the day of Pentecost arrived, they were all together in one place. And suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind.' The outpouring of the Holy Spirit fulfilled the Feast of Weeks to the day, exactly 50 days after Christ's resurrection-Sunday.
17: A direct type: 'You shall bring from your dwelling places two loaves of bread to be waved, made of two tenths of an ephah. They shall be of fine flour, and they shall be baked with leaven, as firstfruits to YHWH.' Pentecost's grain offering uniquely uses leavened bread — unlike every other grain offering. The two leavened loaves may represent the church assembled at Pentecost: Jews and Gentiles (or Jewish and Gentile churches), still imperfect, still containing leaven (sin), yet accepted as firstfruits of the Spirit's harvest. The Pentecost assembly includes sinners made acceptable through Christ's atonement.
18: A shadow: Seven lambs, one bull, two rams as accompanying burnt offerings at Pentecost. The comprehensive array of burnt offerings on Pentecost — the fullest accompanying sacrifice in the entire festival calendar — reflects the completeness of the Spirit's coming: YHWH giving fully of himself at this appointed meeting.
19: A direct type: 'And you shall offer one male goat for a sin offering, and two male lambs a year old as a sacrifice of peace offerings.' The Pentecost offering includes a sin offering and peace offerings — forgiveness and fellowship accompany the Spirit's arrival. Acts 2:38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' Pentecost brought exactly what the accompanying offerings promised: forgiveness (sin offering) and peace (peace offerings) through the Spirit.
20: A shadow: 'And the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering before YHWH, with the two lambs. They shall be holy to YHWH for the priest.' The waving before YHWH — presentation to God — is the central act. The Spirit's descent at Pentecost is God's response to Christ's presentation of his completed sacrifice: 'Being therefore exalted at the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing' (Acts 2:33).
21: A shadow: 'And you shall make a proclamation on the same day. You shall hold a holy convocation. You shall not do any ordinary work. It is a statute forever in all your dwelling places throughout your generations.' The holy convocation on Pentecost corresponds to the gathered church at Acts 2:1 ('they were all together in one place'). The Spirit comes to a gathered, convened assembly — the appointed meeting-time met by a gathered people.
22: A direct type: 'And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge, nor shall you gather the gleanings after your harvest. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner: I am YHWH your God.' The gleaning law appears in the middle of the feast calendar (cf. Leviticus 19:9-10; Deuteronomy 24:19-22). It is not incidental — the feast of Pentecost (harvest festival) is to be accompanied by provision for the poor. The Spirit's outpouring at Pentecost (Acts 2:44-45: 'they were selling their possessions and distributing the proceeds to all') generated exactly this: the church immediately began sharing with the poor. The gleaning principle became the church's practice.
23: A shadow: YHWH spoke again, introducing the fall feasts. The divine speech marks the transition from the spring feasts (fulfilled at Christ's first coming) to the fall feasts (eschatological). Paul and the NT frequently reference the fall feasts in eschatological passages, suggesting their fulfillment lies ahead.
24: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel, saying, In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall observe a day of solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed with blast of trumpets [teruat shofar], a holy convocation.' The Feast of Trumpets (Yom Teruah) announces an imminent sacred assembly. Paul: 'the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God' (1 Thessalonians 4:16). Matthew 24:31: 'he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds.' Revelation 11:15: 'the seventh angel blew his trumpet, and there were loud voices in heaven.' The Feast of Trumpets is the type of the eschatological trumpet that announces Christ's return.
25: A shadow: 'You shall not do any ordinary work, and you shall present a food offering to YHWH.' The cessation of normal work on Yom Teruah signals the interruption of ordinary history by the eschatological event. The Parousia interrupts history: 'the day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night' (1 Thessalonians 5:2).
26: A shadow: YHWH spoke about Yom Kippur. The divine speech introducing the Day of Atonement underscores its weight — this is the most solemn of all the feasts, requiring its own divine address.
27: A direct type: 'Now on the tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. It shall be for you a time of holy convocation, and you shall afflict yourselves and present a food offering to YHWH.' Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement — the OT's most comprehensive atonement ritual. Hebrews 9:1-14 provides the most systematic typological reading of Yom Kippur: the holy of holies → heaven; the high priest → Christ; animal blood → Christ's own blood; annual repetition → once for all. Additionally, Zechariah 12:10: 'when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced, they shall mourn for him, as one mourns for an only child.' The eschatological Yom Kippur for Israel — national mourning at the recognition of Christ — is the fulfillment of the afflicting-of-souls in v27.
28: A direct type: 'And you shall not do any work on that very day, for it is a Day of Atonement, to make atonement for you before YHWH your God.' The Day of Atonement makes atonement — its entire purpose is kippurim. The annual repetition of Yom Kippur was itself a testimony to the inadequacy of the ritual: 'if it could, would they not have ceased to be offered, since the worshipers, having once been cleansed, would no longer have any consciousness of sins?' (Hebrews 10:2). Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the substantive reality that makes the annual shadow unnecessary: 'he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:26).
29: A direct type: 'For whoever is not afflicted on that very day shall be cut off from his people.' Participation in Yom Kippur — acknowledging one's need for atonement, afflicting oneself in repentance — is mandatory. Those who refuse to recognize their sin and need for atonement are cut off. Christ: 'Unless you believe that I am he you will die in your sins' (John 8:24). The Yom Kippur warning against refusing to be afflicted is the OT form of the NT warning against rejecting the atonement.
30: A shadow: 'And whoever does any work on that very day, that person I will destroy from among his people.' The absolute cessation of work on Yom Kippur means that on the day of atonement, human labor makes no contribution. The atonement is entirely God's work, not humanity's. Ephesians 2:8-9: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast.'
31: A shadow: 'You shall not do any work. It is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.' The perpetual Yom Kippur statute was observed for centuries until 70 CE when the temple's destruction made the sacrificial rite impossible. Hebrews was written into exactly this crisis — its argument that Christ's sacrifice supersedes Yom Kippur became immediately practical when the annual rite could no longer be performed.
32: A direct type: 'It is a Sabbath of solemn rest to you, and you shall afflict yourselves. On the ninth day of the month beginning at evening, from evening to evening shall you keep your Sabbath.' Yom Kippur is called a shabbat shabbaton — a Sabbath of Sabbaths, the most complete rest. Christ entered his ultimate rest on the Sabbath in the tomb (Matthew 12:40: 'three days and three nights in the heart of the earth') — the Sabbath between crucifixion and resurrection was the Sabbath of Sabbaths, the total rest of the completed sacrifice.
33: A shadow: YHWH spoke to Moses about the Feast of Tabernacles. The divine address introducing the last and most joyful of the feasts — the harvest festival of dwelling with God — signals the culminating appointment of the divine calendar.
34: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel, saying, On the fifteenth day of this seventh month and for seven days is the Feast of Booths [Sukkot] to YHWH.' The Feast of Tabernacles is the feast of dwelling — Israel dwells in temporary booths, re-enacting the wilderness journey and anticipating final ingathering. John 1:14: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt [eskēnōsen] among us.' The Greek verb is the LXX equivalent of shakan (to tabernacle/dwell as in a tent) — John deliberately invokes the Tabernacles/Sukkot language. The incarnation is the ultimate divine Sukkot: God tabernacling in human flesh.
35: A shadow: 'On the first day shall be a holy convocation; you shall not do any ordinary work.' The opening of Sukkot with a convocation mirrors the church's weekly gathering in Christ's presence — the tabernacle presence that continues through the Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16: 'God's Spirit dwells in you').
36: A direct type: 'For seven days you shall present food offerings to YHWH. On the eighth day you shall hold a holy convocation and present a food offering to YHWH. It is a solemn assembly [atzeret]; you shall not do any ordinary work.' The eighth day Shemini Atzeret — the great assembly at Sukkot's end — is the climax of the entire feast calendar. John 7:37-38: on the last day of the feast, the great day, Jesus stood up and cried out: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, out of his heart will flow rivers of living water.' Jesus announced himself as the fulfillment of Sukkot at the eighth day great assembly — the living water flowing from the rock (Exod 17, 1 Corinthians 10:4) now flowing from him.
37: A shadow: 'These are the appointed feasts of YHWH, which you shall proclaim as times of holy convocation, for presenting to YHWH food offerings, burnt offerings and grain offerings, sacrifices and drink offerings, each on its proper day.' The summary of the feast calendar — all seven feasts are divine appointments, YHWH's scheduled meetings with his people. Colossians 2:17: the body/substance of all these appointments is Christ. Every scheduled divine meeting in the feast calendar is a meeting with Christ: at Passover, at Firstfruits, at Pentecost, and at the eschatological fall feasts.
38: A shadow: 'Besides the Sabbaths of YHWH and besides your gifts and besides all your vow offerings and besides all your freewill offerings, which you give to YHWH.' The feasts are in addition to the weekly Sabbath — they supplement rather than replace the weekly rest. The eschatological rest of Hebrews 4 is the fullness of what weekly Sabbath and the annual feasts only partially anticipated.
39: A shadow: 'On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when you have gathered in the produce of the land, you shall celebrate the feast of YHWH seven days. On the first day shall be a solemn rest, and on the eighth day shall be a solemn rest.' The agricultural timing of Sukkot — after the final harvest — places it as the eschatological feast: all the harvest gathered in, then the great rest. The millennium followed by the eternal Sabbath.
40: A direct type: 'And you shall take on the first day the fruit of splendid trees, branches of palm trees and boughs of leafy trees and willows of the brook, and you shall rejoice before YHWH your God for seven days.' The four Sukkot species — etrog, lulav (palm), myrtle, willow — were carried in procession as signs of joy and victory. At Jesus's triumphal entry, John 12:13: 'they took branches of palm trees and went out to meet him, crying, Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.' The crowd greeted Jesus with Sukkot's palm branches (lulav) — they were performing the Feast of Tabernacles at Passover time, instinctively recognizing their King, though the timing awaited the final fulfillment.
41: A shadow: 'You shall celebrate it as a feast to YHWH for seven days in the year. It is a statute forever throughout your generations; you shall celebrate it in the seventh month.' The perpetual Sukkot anticipates the permanent tabernacling of God with humanity at the end: Revelation 21:3: 'Behold, the dwelling place [skēnē] of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God.' The annual booth-dwelling is the shadow of the eternal dwelling.
42: A direct type: 'You shall dwell in booths for seven days. All native Israelites shall dwell in booths.' The temporary booth (sukkah) re-enacts the wilderness sojourn — dependence, fragility, divine provision. The incarnation was God's entrance into the human sukkah: the Word dwelling in the fragile, temporary structure of human flesh. 2 Corinthians 5:1-4: Paul uses booth/tent imagery for the mortal body; Christ took on exactly this tent-body, to be 'swallowed up by life' at the resurrection.
43: A direct type: '...that your generations may know that I made the people of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt: I am YHWH your God.' The booth-dwelling commemorates the Exodus — dependence on YHWH's provision through the wilderness. Christ accomplished the new Exodus (Luke 9:31: Moses and Elijah spoke of 'his exodus that he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem') and leads his people through the wilderness of this age to the promised rest. The booths of Sukkot are the type of the church in via — dwelling in temporary structures, en route to the eternal city (Hebrews 13:14: 'here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come').
44: A direct type: 'Thus Moses declared to the people of Israel the appointed feasts of YHWH.' Moses, the faithful servant (Hebrews 3:5), declares seven divinely scheduled appointments — each one a meeting with the coming Christ. From Passover (death) through Unleavened Bread (burial), Firstfruits (resurrection), Pentecost (Spirit), Trumpets (return), Yom Kippur (national atonement), and Tabernacles (eternal dwelling) — the entire calendar is one extended Christological prophecy written not in words but in times and seasons. Paul's summary stands over the whole: 'These are a shadow of things to come, but the substance belongs to Christ' (Colossians 2:17).
